- How do the hosepipes get into the tubes?
	 - He cuts the bottom off and glue the tube in the bottom. This is to seal it. He might optimize but that's how it works now.
- How does the bleach agent get in the tubes?
	 - There's bleaching inside the device; and the tubes need to be cleaned between cycles. You backflush the bleach into the tubes, let it sit, let it destroy everything chemically and then go again. It goes through the same hosepipes it comes out of. The distribution valve that has 12 valves e.g. can send the liquid wherever you need be.
 - How often have you do to change the hosepipe setup?
	 - It should last at least a month. When I collected my last, it was 31 / 31 minutes per run and every single one is the same set up. It shouldn't have to change very often.
 - Why does it have to change?
	 - The chip is made out of a palmer and the bleach will degrade it slowly over time. At least a month, but he has the same chip sitting there since December.
- What happens to the samples after the examination/bleaching?
	 - It's a time sink. It's an eight minute duration. You don't want to skimp on it because you don't want to do sample A, send it but you haven't cleaned sample B yet.
- Do you manually place the samples under the microscope and examine them? 
	- It's all an automated process by the microscope and the pump
- How long do the samples survive outside the refrigerator?
	 - At least a day (8h worth of samples is acceptable)
- Could the experiment also run overnight?
	- If you did it late at night (8h)
- Why only every 20 minutes? 
	 - There's loading (5min) and to trap them. 4.20s to bleach them but this can't be sped up. Imaging/scanning takes 30 - 60 seconds. He encodes the microscope to scan but the microscope does it by itself.
 - Can the scanning and pumping process happen in parallel? 
	 - Each of these has to go sequentially and realistically, if you're going to paralyze it it would be so that the chip (glass slide) get a bigger piece of glass and put two chips on a single piece of glass. As you're imaging one, the other one is doing the cleaning / loading.
- How long does scanning take? 
	- 20sec and you skip if it doesn't work
- Is it possible to get a single from microscope that it's completed?
	- Yes. LabView is very compatible with ASC2, USB, etc. and booleans. Almost any kind of connection and sends serial commands to devices. If it can have an ASC2 command or a python command (the robot) could perhaps be triggered by IO. It would be the easiest way for us.
- Why four samples at once? 
	 - The goal would be to get ... the start was 4 because you ... you can buy ones with more but the limit is 12. You can buy 4. Because you're limited by the loading worms the bleaching and the development, there's no benefit of more samples.
- Does it make sense to have 8 valves, bigger pump, etc. in the future that can be adjusted in the future?
	- Yes. The signals are sent from the sensors (for system feedback).Pressure controller (controlled force driving system); stop valves; syringe; and signal system.
- Can you walk us through the process of choosing and preparing the samples for being pipetted?
	- You put aged sequenced worms (embryo, young, young adult, etc.). You sequence them so they're all the same age and then they eat the bacteria and sense it. And the bacteria has the RNA1 in it. It's the genetic farm, and this causes the knockdown of the gene. And then you pull them outside.
- Which samples do you pick in what order?
	- Answered above.
- Is the order just left to right or do you want to have full control of which sample should be processed at a certain time?
	- Answered above.
 - In a perfect world: how best would you like to interact with a robot?
	- The UI; something that can come and say (get pipette, pick sample, load sample, eject sample, repeat. Eight times. That in a coding sense if they say 8times, I'd get it from you guys and do it eight times. 

- Pls give us the right names for this 'things'
	- diagram96-well plate; pipette tip box / pipette tip rack; (3) microsensophuge tip rack.
